draft: Map each control back to the CIA triad from Module 01: what keeps data private (confidentiality), what keeps it correct and accountable (integrity), and what keeps it there at all (availability)?

fields.label: A team lead should see employee names and branches but never salaries, all from the one employee table. What's the standard way to enforce that?
options: Create a view that selects only the safe columns, grant SELECT on the view, and revoke access to the base table
Ask the application to hide the salary column in its UI
Delete the salary column from the employee table
Tell the team lead not to run queries against the salary column

fields.label: Why is a view called an access-control boundary, not just a convenience?
options: It exposes only the columns and rows its definition selects, and the database enforces that — a user granted the view but revoked on the base table literally cannot reach the hidden data
It makes the query run faster by caching the result set on disk
It renames the columns so attackers can't guess what they hold
It encrypts the underlying table so only the view can decrypt it

fields.label: Access control decides who CAN act. What does an audit log add on top of that?
options: A record of who actually DID what, from which account and when — the accountability that makes integrity enforceable, and a legal requirement under HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and SOX
It blocks unauthorized users before they can run a query
It automatically rolls back any change it considers suspicious
It encrypts the data at rest so a stolen backup is useless

fields.label: Match each control to the part of the CIA triad it primarily protects.
fields.help: Confidentiality = kept private; Integrity = correct and accountable; Availability = still there when needed.
